female-38-mdd.name: Charles Harris
female-38-mdd.chiefComplaint: I just feel down all the time

missedConsequences.Bipolar Disorder: Missing mania history leads to inappropriate treatment.

allDiagnoses.name: Major Depressive Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Meets DSM criteria: depressed mood, anhedonia, sleep/appetite changes, fatigue, worthlessness >2 weeks.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypothyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present as depression. Check TSH.

allDiagnoses.name: Bipolar Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must screen for prior mania before treating as MDD.

allDiagnoses.name: Adjustment Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Possible given job loss, but symptoms are severe for adjustment.

female-38-mdd.oldcartsNarrative: "For the past 2 months, I've felt like a cloud is over me. I used to love reading, gardening, time with friends - now I don't enjoy anything. I'm exhausted all the time but can't sleep well. Lost about 10 pounds without trying. I feel worthless and like I'm letting everyone down. Work has been hard - I lost my job a few months ago."

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any thoughts of hurting yourself or not wanting to be alive?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes I think everyone would be better off without me. I haven't made any plans though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: MUST ALWAYS ASK. Passive SI present - need further safety assessment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you thought about how you might hurt yourself? Do you have access to weapons or medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I haven't thought about how. I don't have guns. I have some old pills in the medicine cabinet from a surgery years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing for plan, means, and access - critical safety evaluation. Should recommend removing old medications.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your sleep, appetite, energy, concentration.
historyQuestions.answer: Wake up at 3am can't fall back asleep. No appetite, lost 10 pounds. Exhausted. Can't concentrate on anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sleep (early morning awakening), appetite decrease, energy low, concentration impaired - all MDD criteria.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had periods where you felt the opposite - very high energy, little need for sleep, racing thoughts?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've never felt like that. I've always been pretty even-keeled until this.
historyQuestions.reasoning: MUST SCREEN for mania before treating as MDD. Antidepressants can trigger mania.

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for enjoyment?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing anymore. Things I used to love feel like chores. I've stopped calling friends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ANHEDONIA - cardinal symptom of MDD.

historyQuestions.text: How do you feel about yourself?
historyQuestions.answer: Worthless. I feel like a burden to everyone. I'm letting my family down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Excessive guilt and worthlessness are MDD criteria

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever felt this way before?
historyQuestions.answer: I had a rough patch in my 20s but it wasn't this bad. Never saw anyone for it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior episodes suggest recurrent MDD - affects treatment decisions

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just some vitamins. Nothing prescription. I've never taken antidepressants.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review; first episode requiring treatment

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing antidepressants

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been healthy. Had my thyroid checked a few years ago and it was normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medical history; thyroid will be rechecked to rule out as cause

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol are you drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: More than I should. Maybe a bottle of wine a night. It's the only thing that helps me sleep.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical to assess - alcohol worsens depression and affects treatment

historyQuestions.text: Any other substances - marijuana, opioids, anything else?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just the wine. I don't do drugs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Full substance screen important

historyQuestions.text: Have you experienced any trauma or abuse?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing major. My childhood was okay. No abuse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Trauma history affects treatment approach and may indicate PTSD component

social_life_stressors.answer: Lost my job 3 months ago. Still looking. My savings are running out.
social_life_stressors.relevance: Major life stressor - job loss is common precipitant.

social_support.answer: My husband is supportive but worried. I've been isolating from friends.
social_support.relevance: Has support but withdrawing - important for treatment planning.

social_substance_use.answer: Drinking more wine at night to help me sleep. Maybe a bottle a night now.
social_substance_use.relevance: Increased alcohol use - can worsen depression, need to address.

social_family_history.answer: My mom had depression. My aunt took her own life.
social_family_history.relevance: Family history of depression and suicide increases risk.

physicalExam.finding: Tired-appearing, poor eye contact, psychomotor slowing. Affect flat. Speech soft and slow. Thought content: feels worthless, passive SI, no HI. No psychosis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic depressive presentation - psychomotor retardation, flat affect.

physicalExam.finding: Thyroid normal size, no nodules.
physicalExam.reasoning: Screen for goiter - will also check TSH.

labs.name: TSH
labs.result: TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal 0.4-4.0). Euthyroid - thyroid not contributing to symptoms.
labs.reasoning: ESSENTIAL to rule out hypothyroidism which mimics depression (fatigue, weight gain, cognitive slowing, depressed mood). Always check before starting antidepressants.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: Hgb 13.2, MCV 88, WBC 6.8, Plt 245 - all normal
labs.reasoning: Rules out anemia as cause of fatigue. Macrocytic anemia (high MCV) could suggest B12 deficiency or alcohol-related.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 140, K 4.1, Glucose 92, Ca 9.4, Cr 0.8 - all normal
labs.reasoning: Rules out electrolyte abnormalities, hypercalcemia, and metabolic causes of mood changes.

labs.name: Vitamin B12 and Folate
labs.result: B12 420 pg/mL (normal), Folate 12 ng/mL (normal)
labs.reasoning: B12 deficiency can cause neuropsychiatric symptoms including depression, especially in older adults or with alcohol use.

labs.name: Liver function tests
labs.result: AST 38, ALT 42, GGT 65 (mildly elevated) - consistent with alcohol use
labs.reasoning: Elevated GGT and mild transaminase elevation suggest alcohol effect. Important given heavy alcohol use history.

labs.name: Urine drug screen
labs.result: Consider if concerned about undisclosed substance use
labs.reasoning: Substance use can cause or worsen depressive symptoms. Patient denies illicit drug use but consider if clinical picture warrants.

labs.name: Pregnancy test
labs.result: Negative
labs.reasoning: MUST check before starting antidepressants in reproductive-age women. Affects medication choice.

labs.name: Hemoglobin A1c
labs.result: 5.6% (normal)
labs.reasoning: Diabetes is associated with higher rates of depression. Baseline metabolic screening.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated for typical depression
imaging.result: Imaging not routinely indicated for major depressive disorder without focal neurological findings
imaging.reasoning: MDD is a clinical diagnosis. Imaging only indicated if: new onset in elderly (rule out stroke, tumor), focal neurological signs, atypical features, or concern for organic cause.

imaging.name: MRI brain
imaging.reasoning: Reserve for: late-onset depression (>50), focal deficits, dementia features, atypical presentation. Not needed for typical MDD in younger patient.

procedures.name: PHQ-9 (Depression screening tool)
procedures.result: Score 18/27 - Moderately Severe Depression. Items positive for anhedonia, depressed mood, sleep, fatigue, appetite, concentration, worthlessness. Item 9 (SI): 'Several days'
procedures.reasoning: Validated screening and monitoring tool. Score guides severity: 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20+ severe. Track over time to monitor treatment response.

procedures.name: Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS)
procedures.result: Passive SI without plan, intent, or preparatory behaviors. No prior attempts. Protective factors present (husband, does not want to hurt family).
procedures.reasoning: Structured suicide risk assessment. Documents ideation type, intensity, behaviors, and protective factors. Essential for risk stratification and documentation.

procedures.name: GAD-7 (Anxiety screening)
procedures.result: Score 12 - Moderate anxiety symptoms. Common comorbidity with depression.
procedures.reasoning: Anxiety and depression commonly co-occur. Affects treatment choice - SSRIs treat both. Important to assess.

procedures.name: AUDIT (Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test)
procedures.result: Score 16 - Hazardous alcohol use. Recommend intervention.
procedures.reasoning: Quantifies alcohol use severity. Score >8 suggests hazardous drinking. Important for treatment planning - alcohol worsens depression and interacts with medications.

treatments.name: SSRI antidepressant (sertraline, escitalopram)
treatments.result: Start sertraline 50mg daily. Counsel on 2-4 week onset of action, initial side effects (nausea, headache), and importance of continuation.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment for MDD. SSRIs preferred for safety profile. Sertraline good choice with anxiety comorbidity. Start low, increase every 2-4 weeks until response.

treatments.name: Psychotherapy referral (CBT, IPT)
treatments.result: Referral to therapist for Cognitive Behavioral Therapy. Combined medication + therapy is most effective.
treatments.reasoning: Psychotherapy (especially CBT and IPT) is evidence-based for MDD. COMBINATION of medication + therapy is MORE EFFECTIVE than either alone. Essential referral.

treatments.name: Safety planning for suicidal ideation
treatments.result: Completed safety plan: Warning signs, coping strategies, social contacts, crisis resources (988 Suicide Lifeline), means restriction (no firearms in home). Written copy given to patient.
treatments.reasoning: Safety planning is evidence-based intervention for suicidal ideation. Collaborative, empowering. Provide crisis line numbers (988). Discuss means restriction.

treatments.name: Brief alcohol intervention
treatments.result: Discussed alcohol's effect on depression and sleep. Set goal to reduce to 1-2 drinks max on occasion. Consider AA or outpatient program if unable to cut back.
treatments.reasoning: Alcohol worsens depression, disrupts sleep, and reduces medication effectiveness. Brief intervention can be effective. Consider referral if unable to reduce.

treatments.name: Sleep hygiene counseling
treatments.result: Discussed: consistent sleep schedule, no screens before bed, no alcohol as sleep aid, limit caffeine. Consider trazodone if insomnia persists.
treatments.reasoning: Sleep disruption is both symptom and perpetuating factor in depression. Address sleep to improve outcomes. Trazodone or hydroxyzine can be added for sleep.

treatments.name: Exercise prescription
treatments.result: Encouraged 30 minutes of moderate exercise 3-5x/week. Exercise has antidepressant effects comparable to medication in mild-moderate depression.
treatments.reasoning: Regular exercise has demonstrated antidepressant effects. Recommend starting with walks and building up. Provides structure and behavioral activation.

treatments.name: Avoid benzodiazepines
treatments.result: Benzodiazepines NOT prescribed - risk of dependence, worsens depression, dangerous with alcohol use
treatments.reasoning: Benzodiazepines should be AVOIDED in depression. Worsen depression long-term, dependence risk, dangerous with alcohol. SSRIs treat anxiety component.

consults.name: Mental health therapist (psychologist, LCSW)
consults.result: Referral for individual therapy. CBT or IPT preferred for depression.
consults.reasoning: Psychotherapy is essential component of treatment. Combined treatment more effective than medication alone.

consults.name: Psychiatry referral
consults.result: Not needed initially for uncomplicated MDD. Refer if: treatment-resistant, suicidal, psychotic features, bipolar suspected, complex comorbidities.
consults.reasoning: Primary care can manage uncomplicated MDD. Refer for: no response to 2+ adequate trials, psychotic features, high suicide risk, diagnostic uncertainty, bipolar.

consults.name: Addiction medicine / Substance use treatment
consults.result: Consider if unable to reduce alcohol use with brief intervention
consults.reasoning: If alcohol use doesn't improve with brief intervention, formal substance use treatment may be needed. Addressing alcohol is critical for depression recovery.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with close follow-up
disposition.result: Follow up in 2 weeks to assess medication tolerance, side effects, and symptom trajectory. Sooner if worsening. Safety plan in place.
disposition.reasoning: Close follow-up essential, especially early in treatment. Monitor for response, side effects, worsening SI. Weekly contact initially may be appropriate.

disposition.name: Provide crisis resources
disposition.result: Provided 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, local crisis center number, and ER instructions if thoughts of self-harm worsen.
disposition.reasoning: All patients with any SI should have crisis resources. 988 is national suicide prevention line. Document provision of resources.

disposition.name: Psychiatric hospitalization
disposition.result: NOT indicated currently - passive SI only, no plan/intent, good support, engaged in treatment
disposition.reasoning: Hospitalization for: active suicidal ideation with plan/intent, unable to contract for safety, psychotic features, lack of support, imminent risk. This patient has passive SI with protective factors.

actualDiagnosis.name: Major Depressive Disorder
keyFindings: Depressed mood most days for >2 weeks
Passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent
teachingPoints: ALWAYS ask about suicidal ideation - directly and non-judgmentally
ALWAYS screen for mania before diagnosing MDD - antidepressants can trigger mania
MDD diagnosis: ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks including depressed mood or anhedonia
SIGECAPS: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidality
Check TSH - hypothyroidism mimics depression
Address alcohol use - common comorbidity that worsens depression

guidelineReferences.name: APA Practice Guideline for Treatment of Patients with Major Depressive Disorder (3rd Ed)
keyPoints: SSRIs, SNRIs, mirtazapine, bupropion are first-line pharmacotherapy
Combination of medication + psychotherapy more effective than either alone
CBT and interpersonal therapy have strongest evidence base
Continue treatment 4-9 months after remission to prevent relapse
ECT for treatment-resistant or severe depression with psychotic features

guidelineReferences.name: VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for Management of MDD
keyPoints: PHQ-9 for screening and monitoring (score ≥10 suggests MDD)
Screen for bipolar disorder before starting antidepressants
Measure-based care: track symptoms with validated tool at each visit
If no improvement in 4-8 weeks, optimize dose or switch
Safety planning intervention for all patients with suicidal ideation

phq9.name: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire-9)
phq9.description: Screens for depression severity and monitors treatment response

severe.action: Immediate treatment, consider referral to psychiatry

phq9.note: Question 9 asks about suicidal ideation - any positive response requires direct assessment
phq9.source: Kroenke K et al. J Gen Intern Med. 2001;16(9):606-613

closureScript.diagnosis: You have major depression. It's a real medical condition affecting your brain chemistry, made worse by the stress of losing your job.
closureScript.plan: I want to start you on an antidepressant. It takes 2-4 weeks to work. I'm also referring you to a therapist - combining medication with therapy works best. We need to address the alcohol too, as it's making the depression worse.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: I'm concerned about your thoughts of people being better off without you. I need you to promise you'll call this crisis line or go to the ER if those thoughts get stronger or you think about acting on them. Can you do that?
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You're not weak or lazy. Depression is a real illness. With treatment, most people feel significantly better. I'll see you back in 2 weeks to see how you're doing.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had a plan to harm themselves?
whatIfScenarios.description: Active suicidal ideation with specific plan and access to means

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling really down for the past month
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been thinking about ending it. I have a plan. I know how I'd do it.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Major depression with active suicidal ideation - psychiatric emergency

feedback.correct: Active SI with plan = psychiatric emergency. Do not let patient leave. Remove access to means (guns, pills). Emergent psychiatric evaluation. Likely needs inpatient admission. May require 1:1 observation, possibly involuntary hold if refusing care.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of high-risk patient. What's the disposition?
feedback.incorrect: Passive SI (vague thoughts, no plan) = can often manage outpatient with safety plan. ACTIVE SI with PLAN = psychiatric emergency. Immediate psychiatric evaluation, do not leave patient alone, remove access to lethal means. Hospitalization usually indicated.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Passive SI (wishes they weren't alive, no plan) = outpatient management with safety plan and close follow-up. Active SI with plan = hospitalization needed. Plan + means + intent = highest risk.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was a history of 'really good' periods?
whatIfScenarios.description: Depression with history of decreased sleep, high energy, impulsive spending

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling really down for the past month
chiefComplaint.modified: I'm down now, but sometimes I have periods where I feel amazing - don't need sleep, tons of energy, made some 'investments' that didn't work out...

feedback.correct: ALWAYS screen for mania before starting antidepressant. History of mania = BIPOLAR, not MDD. Antidepressant monotherapy can trigger mania. Treatment: mood stabilizer (lithium, valproate) or atypical antipsychotic, not SSRI alone.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of bipolar features. Why does this change treatment?
feedback.incorrect: CRITICAL: screen for mania before diagnosing MDD. If history of manic episodes (decreased sleep, high energy, impulsivity, grandiosity) = BIPOLAR disorder. Antidepressants can trigger mania in bipolar patients. Need mood stabilizer, not SSRI monotherapy.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Unipolar depression = SSRI is first-line. Bipolar depression = mood stabilizer or atypical antipsychotic, NOT antidepressant monotherapy. Missing bipolar history and giving SSRI can precipitate dangerous mania.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was an 80-year-old with new-onset depression?
whatIfScenarios.description: First depressive episode at age 80 with cognitive changes

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling really down for the past month
chiefComplaint.modified: 80-year-old: I've never felt this way before. I'm also having trouble remembering things.

feedback.correct: New-onset depression in elderly = needs medical workup. Consider: malignancy (pancreatic!), dementia (pseudodementia vs true), vascular disease, hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, medications (beta-blockers, steroids). May need brain MRI. Still treat depression but investigate.
feedback.partial: Good concern for underlying cause. What workup is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Late-onset depression (first episode after 60-65) is MORE LIKELY to have underlying cause. Workup: TSH, B12, CBC, CMP, medication review, consider neuroimaging. 'Pseudodementia' (depression causing cognitive symptoms) vs early dementia. Vascular depression (microvascular disease on MRI) is common.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Young adult with depression = likely primary psychiatric. Elderly with new-onset depression = needs medical/neurological workup. Age of onset changes the differential and required evaluation.

female-29-gad.name: Kimberly Stevens
female-29-gad.chiefComplaint: I can't stop worrying about everything

missedConsequences.Hyperthyroidism: Can present as anxiety. Must check TSH.
missedConsequences.Panic Disorder: Different treatment approach if primarily panic attacks.

commonPitfalls: Not screening for medical causes (thyroid, cardiac)
Missing comorbid depression
Not asking about panic attacks
Missing substance use as contributor

allDiagnoses.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Excessive worry about multiple domains for >6 months with somatic symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Hyperthyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with anxiety, palpitations, tremor. Check TSH.

allDiagnoses.name: Panic Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Has some panic symptoms but primary issue is pervasive worry.

allDiagnoses.name: Caffeine-Induced Anxiety
allDiagnoses.rationale: High caffeine can contribute - should assess.

female-29-gad.oldcartsNarrative: "I've always been a worrier but it's gotten out of control. I worry about work, my relationships, health, finances - everything. My mind won't stop. I'm tense all the time, my heart races, I can't sleep because my brain won't shut off. Sometimes I feel like I can't breathe. This has been going on for at least a year."

historyQuestions.text: What kinds of things do you worry about?
historyQuestions.answer: Everything. Work deadlines even when I'm ahead. My health even though I'm healthy. My relationship even though it's fine. Money even though I'm okay financially.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Excessive worry across MULTIPLE domains - characteristic of GAD.

historyQuestions.text: Do you ever have sudden episodes of intense fear with physical symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes my anxiety peaks and my heart pounds, I feel like I can't breathe. But it's not out of the blue - it's when I'm already worried about something.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Some panic symptoms but situational, not unexpected - fits GAD better.

historyQuestions.text: How does the anxiety affect you physically?
historyQuestions.answer: Muscle tension, especially neck and shoulders. Headaches. Trouble sleeping. Heart racing. Stomach issues.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Somatic symptoms of anxiety - muscle tension, insomnia, autonomic symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: Have you felt down or depressed?
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly yes. The constant worry is exhausting. I don't enjoy things like I used to.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screen for comorbid depression - very common with anxiety.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any thoughts of hurting yourself?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that. I worry about dying from something, but I don't want to hurt myself.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Always screen for suicidal ideation in mental health presentations

historyQuestions.text: How long has this been going on?
historyQuestions.answer: I've always been a worrier, but the last year or two it's gotten out of control. Can't remember the last time I felt relaxed.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GAD requires symptoms >6 months - established here

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing prescription. I take some supplements and melatonin to try to sleep.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review before prescribing anxiolytics/antidepressants

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. Had a physical last year and everything was normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms no medical conditions causing anxiety symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Any alcohol or drug use?
historyQuestions.answer: A couple glasses of wine on weekends. No drugs. Lots of coffee though - maybe 5-6 cups a day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Substances can cause/worsen anxiety - caffeine is major contributor here

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of anxiety or mental health issues?
historyQuestions.answer: My mom is a worrier too. My dad had depression at one point.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of anxiety/depression increases risk

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your work and relationships?
historyQuestions.answer: Work is suffering - I second-guess every decision. My boyfriend gets frustrated that I need constant reassurance.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing functional impairment to guide treatment intensity

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever seen anyone for anxiety before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I kept thinking I could handle it on my own. But I can't.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Treatment-naive - starting fresh with therapy and medication options

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_caffeine.answer: I drink a lot of coffee. Maybe 5-6 cups a day. It's the only thing that keeps me going.
social_caffeine.relevance: High caffeine worsens anxiety - important modifiable factor.

occupation_work_stress.answer: High-pressure job in finance. Always deadlines, always performance pressure.

social_sleep.answer: Takes forever to fall asleep. Mind races. Maybe 5 hours a night.

social_exercise.answer: No time. I know I should but I'm too busy being anxious about everything.
social_exercise.relevance: Exercise is effective anxiety treatment - important target.

physicalExam.finding: Appears tense, fidgeting. Rapid speech. Alert and oriented. No psychosis. Denies SI.
physicalExam.reasoning: Visible anxiety - tension, fidgeting, rapid speech.

physicalExam.finding: Normal size, no nodules, no bruit.
physicalExam.reasoning: Screen for thyroid pathology.

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic but regular. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mildly elevated HR consistent with anxious state.

labs.name: TSH
labs.result: TSH 1.8 mIU/L (normal). Rules out thyroid dysfunction as cause of anxiety symptoms.
labs.reasoning: ESSENTIAL - hyperthyroidism can present identically to anxiety (palpitations, tremor, weight loss, irritability). Must check in all new anxiety presentations.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: Normal - no anemia contributing to symptoms
labs.reasoning: Anemia can cause fatigue and palpitations. Quick screen to rule out.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Normal electrolytes, glucose 92
labs.reasoning: Electrolyte abnormalities and hypoglycemia can cause anxiety-like symptoms.

labs.name: Urine drug screen
labs.result: Consider if concerned about stimulant use
labs.reasoning: Stimulants (cocaine, amphetamines) cause anxiety symptoms. Patient denies drug use but consider if clinical picture warrants.

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Sinus tachycardia at 88 bpm. Normal intervals. No ectopy. No ischemic changes.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out arrhythmia as cause of palpitations. Sinus tachycardia is consistent with anxiety. Would identify SVT, afib, or other arrhythmias if present.

imaging.name: Holter monitor
imaging.result: Not routinely indicated for anxiety with normal ECG
imaging.reasoning: Consider only if palpitations occur in discrete episodes with concerning features (syncope, near-syncope) or abnormal ECG. Not needed for typical anxiety presentation.

procedures.name: GAD-7 (Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale)
procedures.result: Score 16/21 - Severe anxiety. Items positive for: uncontrollable worry, difficulty relaxing, restlessness, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance, fear of something awful happening.
procedures.reasoning: Validated screening and monitoring tool for GAD. Score 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15+ severe. Track over time to monitor treatment response.

procedures.name: PHQ-9 (Depression screen)
procedures.result: Score 10/27 - Moderate depressive symptoms. Comorbid depression present.
procedures.reasoning: Anxiety and depression commonly co-occur (50%+ of GAD patients have comorbid depression). Affects treatment approach. SSRIs treat both.

procedures.name: Screen for panic attacks
procedures.result: No discrete, unexpected panic attacks. Anxiety symptoms are situational and tied to worry rather than spontaneous.
procedures.reasoning: Distinguish GAD from Panic Disorder - different treatment nuances. GAD: pervasive worry with somatic symptoms. Panic: discrete unexpected episodes.

treatments.name: SSRI antidepressant (sertraline, escitalopram)
treatments.result: Start escitalopram 5mg daily, increase to 10mg after 1 week. First-line for GAD. Takes 2-4 weeks for anxiolytic effect.
treatments.reasoning: SSRIs are FIRST-LINE for GAD. Escitalopram and sertraline have best evidence. Start low to avoid initial activation/jitteriness. Full effect takes 4-6 weeks.

treatments.name: SNRI (venlafaxine, duloxetine)
treatments.result: Alternative first-line option - venlafaxine XR or duloxetine
treatments.reasoning: SNRIs also first-line for GAD. May be preferred if also treating chronic pain (duloxetine). Venlafaxine requires BP monitoring at higher doses.

treatments.name: Buspirone
treatments.result: Consider as adjunct or alternative - 5mg TID, titrate up. Takes 2-4 weeks to work.
treatments.reasoning: Non-benzodiazepine anxiolytic. Can augment SSRI or use alone. No dependence risk. Slow onset (2-4 weeks). Requires TID dosing.

treatments.name: Psychotherapy referral (CBT)
treatments.result: Referral for Cognitive Behavioral Therapy - first-line treatment for GAD. Combined with medication is most effective.
treatments.reasoning: CBT is FIRST-LINE for GAD. As effective as medication, more durable effects. Combination therapy most effective. Specifically addresses maladaptive thought patterns.

treatments.name: Caffeine reduction counseling
treatments.result: Counseled to reduce caffeine from 5-6 cups to 1-2 cups daily. Taper gradually to avoid withdrawal headache. Caffeine significantly worsens anxiety.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL intervention - 5-6 cups of coffee significantly worsens anxiety. Caffeine is anxiogenic. Reducing caffeine can substantially improve symptoms. Taper to avoid withdrawal.

treatments.name: Sleep hygiene counseling
treatments.result: Discussed: consistent sleep schedule, wind-down routine, no screens before bed, limit caffeine after noon, relaxation techniques before bed.
treatments.reasoning: Sleep deprivation worsens anxiety. Breaking the cycle of insomnia-anxiety-insomnia is critical. Sleep hygiene is first-line intervention.

treatments.name: Exercise prescription
treatments.result: Regular aerobic exercise 30 min 3-5x/week. Proven anxiolytic effect comparable to medication.
treatments.reasoning: Exercise has robust evidence for anxiety reduction. Comparable effect size to medication. Important lifestyle modification.

treatments.name: Relaxation techniques
treatments.result: Taught diaphragmatic breathing. Recommended mindfulness apps (Headspace, Calm). Progressive muscle relaxation.
treatments.reasoning: Relaxation training is evidence-based for anxiety. Diaphragmatic breathing activates parasympathetic nervous system. Can use in acute anxiety moments.

treatments.name: Avoid benzodiazepines for chronic use
treatments.result: Benzodiazepines NOT prescribed for long-term use. Risk of dependence, cognitive impairment, withdrawal, rebound anxiety.
treatments.reasoning: Benzodiazepines should NOT be first-line for GAD. Risk of dependence, tolerance, cognitive effects, withdrawal. Use only for short-term bridge while SSRI takes effect, if at all.

treatments.name: PRN benzodiazepine (limited, if severe)
treatments.result: Consider short-term lorazepam 0.5mg PRN only for severe anticipatory anxiety while SSRI takes effect. Dispense limited quantity. Clear expectations.
treatments.reasoning: If anxiety is severe and impairing, short-term benzo while waiting for SSRI effect may be appropriate. Strict limits: 2-4 weeks max, small quantities, clear taper plan.

consults.name: Mental health therapist (CBT)
consults.result: Referral for CBT - evidence-based treatment for GAD
consults.reasoning: CBT is first-line. Combined with medication is most effective. Addresses cognitive distortions and avoidance behaviors.

consults.name: Psychiatry referral
consults.result: Not needed for uncomplicated GAD. Primary care can manage initial treatment.
consults.reasoning: Primary care can manage typical GAD. Refer for: treatment resistance, complex comorbidities, diagnostic uncertainty, severe functional impairment.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with follow-up
disposition.result: Follow up in 2-4 weeks to assess medication tolerance and symptom response. Therapy referral placed. Lifestyle modifications reviewed.
disposition.reasoning: GAD is managed outpatient. Close follow-up to assess response and adjust treatment. Monitor for SSRI side effects and effectiveness.

actualDiagnosis.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder
keyFindings: Excessive worry about multiple domains for >6 months
teachingPoints: GAD = excessive worry about MULTIPLE things for ≥6 months
GAD has somatic component: muscle tension, sleep issues, restlessness, fatigue
Always check TSH - hyperthyroidism mimics anxiety
Assess caffeine intake - major modifiable contributor
Screen for comorbid depression - >50% have both
First-line treatment: SSRIs/SNRIs and/or CBT. Avoid benzos long-term.

gad7.name: GAD-7 (Generalized Anxiety Disorder 7-item scale)
gad7.description: Screens for and monitors severity of generalized anxiety disorder
items: Feeling nervous, anxious, or on edge
Not being able to stop or control worrying
Worrying too much about different things
Being so restless that it's hard to sit still
Feeling afraid as if something awful might happen
gad7.scoringNote: Each item scored 0 (not at all) to 3 (nearly every day). Total 0-21.

thisPatient.clinicalAction: Score 15+ indicates severe anxiety. Recommend SSRI/SNRI + CBT referral. Monitor response with serial GAD-7.

mild.action: Mild anxiety - watchful waiting, consider CBT

moderate.action: Moderate anxiety - treatment indicated (CBT and/or medication)

severe.action: Severe anxiety - active treatment required (medication + therapy)

gad7.source: Spitzer RL et al. Arch Intern Med. 2006;166(10):1092-1097

closureScript.diagnosis: You have generalized anxiety disorder - your brain's alarm system is stuck in overdrive, causing constant worry and physical symptoms.
closureScript.plan: I'd like to start you on a medication that works well for anxiety. I'm also referring you for cognitive behavioral therapy - it's very effective. We need to cut back your caffeine significantly - that's making things worse. And exercise, even 30 minutes of walking, really helps.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: The medication takes 2-4 weeks to work fully and might slightly increase anxiety initially. If you feel significantly worse or have thoughts of hurting yourself, call me immediately.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This is very treatable. Most people feel much better with the right combination of medication and therapy. You don't have to feel this way.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there were discrete panic attacks?
whatIfScenarios.description: Sudden episodes of terror with chest pounding, can't breathe, fear of dying

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling anxious and worried all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: I get these sudden attacks out of nowhere - my heart pounds, I can't breathe, I think I'm dying. They last about 10 minutes.

feedback.correct: Panic disorder = discrete attacks with sudden intense fear + physical symptoms (palpitations, SOB, chest pain, sweating). Rule out cardiac (ECG, especially in older patients) and thyroid. Treatment: SSRI + CBT (exposure therapy). PRN benzos for attacks if severe.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of panic disorder. What distinguishes it from GAD?
feedback.incorrect: Panic disorder: recurrent UNEXPECTED panic attacks (sudden surge of intense fear + physical symptoms, peak within minutes). Different from GAD (constant worry). Treatment: SSRI for prevention, CBT (exposure therapy). Rule out cardiac/thyroid causes.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: GAD = constant low-level worry about many things. Panic disorder = discrete episodes of intense terror that peak quickly. Different patterns require different treatment approaches.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if TSH was low?
whatIfScenarios.description: Anxiety symptoms with tremor, weight loss, and heat intolerance

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling anxious and worried all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been anxious and jittery. I've also lost 10 pounds and feel hot all the time. My hands shake.

feedback.correct: Low TSH with anxiety symptoms = HYPERTHYROIDISM, not primary anxiety. Check free T4/T3, TSI if Graves suspected. Treatment: beta-blocker for symptoms, then methimazole or PTU. Endocrine referral.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of thyroid cause. How does treatment differ?
feedback.incorrect: Hyperthyroidism mimics anxiety: palpitations, tremor, weight loss, heat intolerance, hyperreflexia, tachycardia. ALWAYS check TSH in anxiety presentations. Treatment is thyroid-directed (antithyroid drugs), not psychiatric. SSRIs won't fix this.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Primary anxiety = normal TSH, no weight/temperature changes. Hyperthyroidism = low TSH, weight loss, heat intolerance. Treatment is completely different - thyroid vs psychiatric.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was recent cocaine use?
whatIfScenarios.description: Anxiety with history of stimulant use

chiefComplaint.original: I've been feeling anxious and worried all the time
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been really anxious and jittery. I should mention I used cocaine a few times last month.

feedback.correct: Substance-induced anxiety: symptoms directly caused by intoxication or withdrawal. Can't diagnose primary anxiety disorder until patient is abstinent and symptoms persist. Address substance use first. Consider SUD treatment.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of substance contribution. When can primary anxiety be diagnosed?
feedback.incorrect: With active substance use, can't diagnose primary anxiety disorder - symptoms may be substance-induced. Need period of sustained abstinence (usually 4 weeks) before diagnosing GAD/panic. Address substance use first. Anxiety may resolve completely with abstinence.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Primary anxiety = symptoms present without substance use. Substance-induced = symptoms during intoxication or withdrawal. Must establish abstinence before diagnosing primary disorder.

Energy deficiency is the root cause driving amenorrhea and bone loss

guidelineReferences.name: ASAM Alcohol Withdrawal Management Guideline 2020
keyPoints: Screen ALL hospitalized patients for alcohol use disorder
PAWSS (Prediction of Alcohol Withdrawal Severity Scale) for risk assessment
Symptom-triggered therapy with benzodiazepines preferred over fixed-dose
CIWA-Ar monitoring every 1-4 hours; treat when score ≥8-10
Thiamine 100-500mg IV/IM BEFORE glucose to prevent Wernicke's
Delirium tremens: ICU care, high-dose benzodiazepines, mortality 5-15% untreated

alcohol_use_disorder.name: Alcohol Use Disorder

doorNote.chiefComplaint: I need a refill on my blood pressure medication
doorNote.nurseNotes: Patient here for routine visit. Hypertension noted. Appears slightly anxious. Spouse in waiting room mentioned concerns about his drinking.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me how your blood pressure has been. Are you taking your medication regularly?
historyQuestions.answer: I try to take it most days. Sometimes I forget, especially on weekends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication adherence often affected by substance use

historyQuestions.text: I like to ask all my patients about alcohol. Do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, I have a few drinks. Mostly beer. It helps me relax after work.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-judgmental opener to assess alcohol use

historyQuestions.text: On a typical week, how many days do you drink, and how much on those days?
historyQuestions.answer: I probably drink 5-6 days a week. Usually 4-6 beers, sometimes more on weekends. Maybe a 12-pack on Saturday.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Quantifying use - this exceeds recommended limits significantly

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever felt you should Cut down on your drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: My wife thinks I should. I've tried a few times but it never lasts long.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CAGE question #1 - positive response

historyQuestions.text: Have people Annoyed you by criticizing your drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, my wife is always on my case about it. My doctor mentioned it last time too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CAGE question #2 - positive response

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever felt Guilty about your drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: Sometimes. I missed my daughter's soccer game last month because I was hungover. Felt terrible about that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CAGE question #3 - positive response with functional impairment

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had a drink first thing in the morning to steady your nerves or get rid of a hangover (Eye-opener)?
historyQuestions.answer: Not usually... well, maybe on weekends if I overdid it the night before. A bloody mary helps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CAGE question #4 - positive suggests physical dependence

historyQuestions.text: Do you find you need more alcohol to get the same effect than you used to?
historyQuestions.answer: Definitely. When I was younger, 2-3 beers would give me a buzz. Now I need 6 or more to feel anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Tolerance is criterion for alcohol use disorder

historyQuestions.text: When you don't drink for a day or two, do you notice any symptoms - shakiness, sweating, anxiety?
historyQuestions.answer: If I don't drink by evening, I get kind of jittery and can't sleep. Maybe a little sweaty.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Withdrawal symptoms indicate physical dependence - important for management

historyQuestions.text: Has drinking caused any problems at work or home?
historyQuestions.answer: I called in sick a few times when I was too hungover. My wife and I fight about it. She threatened to leave last month.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Continued use despite negative consequences is key criterion

historyQuestions.text: Do you ever have blackouts - times when you can't remember what happened while drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: Once in a while on big drinking nights. My wife will tell me about conversations I don't remember.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Blackouts indicate heavy episodic use

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had any legal problems related to drinking? DUI?
historyQuestions.answer: I got a DUI about 5 years ago. Lost my license for 6 months. That was a wake-up call but... here I am.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Legal consequences significant indicator

historyQuestions.text: Do you want to cut back or quit drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: Part of me does. I know it's causing problems. But it's hard - it's how I unwind, how I socialize. I don't know who I am without it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing readiness for change - important for treatment planning

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 148/94 (elevated), HR 92, RR 16. Mild tremor noted in outstretched hands.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypertension and tremor can be alcohol-related

physicalExam.finding: Middle-aged male, slightly flushed face, appears mildly anxious. Appropriate dress and grooming. Alcohol odor not detected.
physicalExam.reasoning: Facial flushing and anxiety can be chronic alcohol signs

physicalExam.finding: Mild scleral injection. Enlarged parotid glands bilaterally. Mild glossitis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Parotid enlargement and glossitis are chronic alcohol stigmata

physicalExam.finding: Mild RUQ tenderness, liver edge palpable 2cm below costal margin, smooth. No splenomegaly or ascites.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hepatomegaly with tenderness suggests alcoholic hepatitis/steatosis

physicalExam.finding: Several spider angiomata on upper chest. Palmar erythema noted.
physicalExam.reasoning: Spider angiomata and palmar erythema are signs of chronic liver disease

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented. Mild intention tremor. Tandem gait slightly unsteady. Decreased vibration sense in feet bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Peripheral neuropathy and cerebellar signs from chronic alcohol

differentials.name: Essential Hypertension
keyFeatures: Present but likely worsened by alcohol

alcohol_use_disorder.teachingPoint: CAGE score ≥2 is 93% sensitive for alcohol use disorder. This patient scores 4/4. DSM-5 criteria: ≥2 of 11 criteria in past year = AUD (2-3 mild, 4-5 moderate, ≥6 severe). Key management: 1) Assess withdrawal risk (this patient IS at risk - do not abruptly stop), 2) Brief intervention using motivational interviewing, 3) Offer treatment options (medical management, counseling, AA), 4) Treat comorbidities, 5) Order labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, GGT, CDT). Consider gabapentin or naltrexone for craving reduction.
alcohol_use_disorder.clinicalPearl: This patient came in for blood pressure medication but has severe AUD with physical dependence and liver disease. The 'routine visit' is often where we discover these problems. Screen everyone! And remember - this patient cannot safely stop drinking abruptly due to withdrawal risk.

withdrawal_risk.name: Alcohol Withdrawal

doorNote.chiefComplaint: I feel terrible and I'm shaking
doorNote.nurseNotes: Patient tremulous, diaphoretic, anxious. Reports he ran out of alcohol 2 days ago and couldn't afford to buy more. History of heavy drinking. Last drink approximately 36 hours ago.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last alcoholic drink?
historyQuestions.answer: About 36 hours ago. I ran out of money and couldn't buy more.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timing is critical - withdrawal peaks 24-72 hours after last drink

historyQuestions.text: How much do you usually drink?
historyQuestions.answer: A pint of vodka a day, sometimes more. Been drinking like this for about 10 years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy chronic use increases withdrawal severity

historyQuestions.text: What symptoms are you experiencing right now?
historyQuestions.answer: I can't stop shaking. My heart is pounding. I'm sweating like crazy and feel like I'm going to jump out of my skin. Couldn't sleep at all last night.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic autonomic and neurologic withdrawal symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Are you seeing or hearing anything unusual?
historyQuestions.answer: I keep seeing shadows moving in the corner of my eye. And I hear my phone ringing but it's not. It's scaring me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hallucinations indicate severe withdrawal - DT risk

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had a seizure when you stopped drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, about 2 years ago. I was in the hospital for it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior withdrawal seizures dramatically increase risk of future seizures

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had delirium tremens - confusion, very high fever, severe shaking when stopping?
historyQuestions.answer: The time I had the seizure, they said I had DTs. I was in the ICU for a few days. Don't remember much of it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior DTs = very high risk for recurrence

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: I have liver problems from drinking. And high blood pressure. I'm supposed to take pills but I don't always.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Comorbidities affect management

historyQuestions.text: Have you been sick recently? Any infections?
historyQuestions.answer: I've had a cough for a week or so. Been feeling run down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Concurrent infection can worsen withdrawal and is common in this population

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't know... couple days ago maybe? I've just been drinking.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Malnutrition + alcohol = thiamine deficiency = Wernicke risk

physicalExam.finding: BP 168/102, HR 112, RR 20, Temp 99.8°F. Marked diaphoresis.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypertension, tachycardia, hyperthermia = autonomic hyperactivity

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic, visibly tremulous, anxious and agitated. Poor hygiene.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic withdrawal appearance

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented x3 but mildly confused on detailed questioning. Coarse tremor of hands. Hyperreflexia. No asterixis. Eyes darting around room.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confusion and hyperreflexia indicate severe withdrawal

physicalExam.finding: Pupils 5mm bilaterally, reactive. No nystagmus. Scleral icterus present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Checking for Wernicke (nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia) and liver disease

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Autonomic hyperactivity

physicalExam.finding: Distended, hepatomegaly with RUQ tenderness, no definite ascites. Spleen not palpable.
physicalExam.reasoning: Chronic liver disease assessment

physicalExam.finding: Jaundiced, multiple spider angiomata, palmar erythema, diaphoretic.
physicalExam.reasoning: Stigmata of chronic liver disease

differentials.name: Wernicke Encephalopathy
keyFeatures: Need to give thiamine BEFORE glucose

withdrawal_risk.correctDiagnosis: Severe Alcohol Withdrawal with High DT Risk
withdrawal_risk.teachingPoint: This is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. High-risk features: prior DTs, prior withdrawal seizures, heavy prolonged use, concurrent illness, abnormal LFTs. Management: 1) THIAMINE FIRST (100mg IV) before any glucose, 2) Benzodiazepines (symptom-triggered CIWA protocol or fixed schedule), 3) Aggressive fluid/electrolyte replacement, 4) Admit to monitored bed (likely ICU), 5) Look for concurrent infections. CIWA-Ar score guides benzos. Phenobarbital or propofol if refractory. This patient may seize - have airway equipment ready.
withdrawal_risk.clinicalPearl: The '3 Ds' of alcohol withdrawal: Diaphoresis, Delirium, Death (if untreated). This patient has ALL the high-risk features: prior DTs, prior seizures, hallucinations starting, heavy use. He needs ICU admission and aggressive benzodiazepine treatment. Never underestimate alcohol withdrawal - it kills.

si_assessment.name: Suicidal Ideation Assessment

doorNote.nurseNotes: Patient tearful during intake. When asked screening questions, she disclosed thoughts of 'not wanting to be here anymore.' Husband is in waiting room, appears concerned. Patient agreed to speak with provider.

historyQuestions.text: Can you tell me more about what's been going on?
historyQuestions.answer: Everything just feels hopeless. I've been depressed for months and it keeps getting worse. I don't see the point anymore.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Open-ended start allows patient to share at their own pace

historyQuestions.text: When you say you don't see the point, are you having thoughts of suicide or hurting yourself?
historyQuestions.answer: (Pauses, tears up) Yes. I've been thinking about it a lot. I think everyone would be better off without me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Direct question about suicide is essential - patients rarely volunteer this information and asking does NOT increase risk

historyQuestions.text: Have you thought about how you would hurt yourself? Do you have a plan?
historyQuestions.answer: I've thought about taking pills. We have a lot of medications in the house from various things. I've looked at them.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Specific plan with available means = HIGH RISK

historyQuestions.text: How close have you come to acting on these thoughts?
historyQuestions.answer: A few nights ago I held the pill bottles. I didn't take any but I sat there for an hour thinking about it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rehearsal behavior indicates serious intent

historyQuestions.text: How long have you been having these thoughts?
historyQuestions.answer: The really bad thoughts started about 3 weeks ago. Before that I was depressed but didn't want to die.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Temporal course helps assess trajectory and treatment planning

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you have access to at home?
historyQuestions.answer: My mother's old pain medication - I think oxycodone. Some of my husband's blood pressure pills. Tylenol PM. Lots of stuff.
linkedDx: Suicidal Ideation with Access to Means
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL - access to lethal means requires intervention

historyQuestions.text: What has kept you from acting on these thoughts?
historyQuestions.answer: My kids. I have two little girls. The thought of them growing up without a mom... (crying) But sometimes I think they'd be better off.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Ambivalence and protective factors important for safety planning but diminishing protective thoughts is concerning

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever attempted suicide before?
historyQuestions.answer: I overdosed in college when I was 20. I was hospitalized. That was before I had kids.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior attempts are one of the strongest predictors of future attempts

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about your depression - sleep, appetite, energy, concentration?
historyQuestions.answer: I can't sleep, maybe 3-4 hours a night. I've lost 15 pounds because I have no appetite. I can't concentrate at work. I have no energy for anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing depression severity - severe symptoms contribute to suicide risk

historyQuestions.text: Did something happen that made things worse recently?
historyQuestions.answer: I found out my husband had an affair. He says it's over but I can't trust anything anymore. Everything I believed in is a lie.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Identifying precipitants helps with treatment and safety planning

historyQuestions.text: Who knows what you're going through? Who can you talk to?
historyQuestions.answer: My husband knows I'm depressed but I haven't told him about the suicidal thoughts. I don't want to burden anyone. I've pulled away from my friends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Social isolation increases risk; involving supports important for safety

historyQuestions.text: Have you been using alcohol or any drugs?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been drinking more - maybe a bottle of wine most nights to try to sleep. I don't use drugs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol use increases impulsivity and suicide risk

historyQuestions.text: Have you been getting any treatment - medications or therapy?
historyQuestions.answer: My doctor started me on Lexapro 3 weeks ago but I don't think it's working. I haven't seen a therapist.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Early SSRI treatment may initially increase activation before mood improves - careful monitoring period

historyQuestions.text: Would you be willing to work with me on a plan to keep you safe?
historyQuestions.answer: I... I don't know. I guess so. I don't really want to die, I just want the pain to stop.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Engagement in safety planning crucial; ambivalence is workable

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 128/82, HR 88. Vitals stable.
physicalExam.reasoning: Medical stability allows focus on psychiatric emergency

physicalExam.finding: Tearful, poor eye contact, psychomotor retardation. Dressed casually but appropriately. No evidence of self-harm visible on arms.
physicalExam.reasoning: Mental status begins with observation

physicalExam.finding: Soft, slow speech. Long latency to response. Monotone.
physicalExam.reasoning: Psychomotor slowing consistent with severe depression

physicalExam.finding: Mood: 'Hopeless, empty.' Affect: Constricted, tearful, mood-congruent.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hopelessness is key suicide risk factor

physicalExam.finding: Linear but impoverished. No loosening of associations.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out psychotic process

physicalExam.finding: Suicidal ideation with plan (medication overdose), intent (rehearsal behavior), and means (access to medications). Feelings of worthlessness and guilt. No homicidal ideation. No psychotic symptoms.
linkedDx: Suicidal Ideation with Plan and Intent
physicalExam.reasoning: Comprehensive suicide risk assessment

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented x3. Concentration impaired - difficulty with serial 7s. Memory intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: Depression can impair concentration; rules out delirium

physicalExam.finding: Insight: Recognizes she is depressed and needs help. Judgment: Impaired - accessing means without intervention.
physicalExam.reasoning: Some insight is positive but judgment impaired by depression

Adjustment Disorder with Depressed Mood
keyFeatures: Precipitant identified but symptoms too severe and SI too serious

differentials.name: Bipolar Depression
keyFeatures: Would need history of manic episodes - not elicited

si_assessment.correctDiagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder, Severe, with Suicidal Ideation
si_assessment.teachingPoint: This patient is HIGH RISK for suicide attempt and likely needs inpatient hospitalization. Risk factors: prior attempt, current plan with access to means, hopelessness, impulsivity (alcohol), recent stressor, social isolation, early antidepressant treatment. SAFETY INTERVENTIONS: 1) Do not leave patient alone, 2) Means restriction (involve husband to secure medications), 3) Discuss hospitalization - voluntary vs involuntary, 4) If discharged (not recommended here), safety plan must include crisis numbers, removing access to means, and close follow-up. Document thoroughly.
si_assessment.clinicalPearl: Remember: ASKING about suicide does NOT increase risk - it opens dialogue and can be lifesaving. This patient has multiple high-risk features and needs inpatient psychiatric admission. The ambivalence she expresses ('I don't want to die, I want the pain to stop') is actually a therapeutic opening but does not reduce the acute risk.

guidelineReferences.name: ASAM Clinical Practice Guideline on Alcohol Withdrawal Management
keyPoints: ASSESSMENT: Screen ALL patients for alcohol use; assess withdrawal risk with PAWSS (Prediction of Alcohol Withdrawal Severity Scale) for at-risk patients
MILD-MODERATE WITHDRAWAL (CIWA-Ar <15): Symptom-triggered therapy preferred over fixed-dose; reduces medication use and treatment duration
SEVERE WITHDRAWAL (CIWA-Ar ≥15): May require fixed-dosing or front-loading approach with close monitoring
BENZODIAZEPINES: First-line for AWS; no single agent proven superior; long-acting (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) vs short-acting (lorazepam) based on liver function
PHENOBARBITAL: Consider as adjunct or alternative for refractory withdrawal or benzodiazepine-resistant cases
THIAMINE: Give 100-500mg IV/IM BEFORE glucose in ALL patients with alcohol use disorder to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy
CIWA-Ar MONITORING: Score every 1-4 hours; treat when score ≥8-10; goal is light sedation and symptom control
DELIRIUM TREMENS (DT): Medical emergency - ICU level care, high-dose benzodiazepines, may need IV phenobarbital or propofol
SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS: Benzodiazepines prevent withdrawal seizures; phenytoin is NOT effective for AWS seizures
COMPLICATIONS: DT has 5-15% mortality untreated; monitor for aspiration, arrhythmias, trauma, Wernicke's
MAGNESIUM: Replete if low; common in chronic alcohol use
FLUID/ELECTROLYTES: Monitor and replace; patients often volume depleted with electrolyte abnormalities

procedures.name: Safety planning
procedures.result: NOT a substitute for hospitalization in this case. Patient unable to contract for safety and has active plan with timeline.
procedures.reasoning: Safety planning is useful for lower-risk patients. NOT appropriate when patient has active plan, intent, and timeline. Safety contracts have NO evidence of efficacy.

procedures.name: Collateral information
procedures.result: Brother contacted and confirmed patient's history. Corroborates hopelessness, withdrawal, alcohol use. Agrees patient needs hospitalization. Willing to remove firearms.
procedures.reasoning: Collateral from family/friends provides critical information and supports safety planning. Involves support system in treatment.

treatments.name: Inpatient Psychiatric Hospitalization
treatments.result: VOLUNTARY admission to psychiatric unit. If patient refuses, involuntary hold (varies by state: 5150 in CA, 302 in PA, etc.) is warranted given imminent danger to self.
treatments.reasoning: REQUIRED for this patient. Active suicidal ideation with specific plan, intent, timeline, access to lethal means, and preparatory behavior. This is a psychiatric emergency.

treatments.name: 1:1 constant observation
treatments.result: Placed on 1:1 continuous observation pending psychiatric admission
treatments.reasoning: Patient at imminent risk must be continuously observed until in secure psychiatric setting. Not safe to leave unattended.

treatments.name: Medication initiation
treatments.result: Defer antidepressant initiation to inpatient setting where monitoring is possible
treatments.reasoning: SSRIs may increase activation and SI initially in some patients. Better to initiate in monitored setting. May start after stabilization.

treatments.name: Alcohol withdrawal monitoring/treatment
treatments.result: Monitor for withdrawal symptoms. CIWA protocol if indicated. Benzodiazepines as needed for withdrawal.
treatments.reasoning: Patient is acutely intoxicated and uses alcohol heavily. Will need withdrawal monitoring and possible treatment as BAC drops.

consults.name: Psychiatry consultation - STAT
consults.result: STAT psychiatry consult for evaluation and admission
consults.reasoning: Psychiatric evaluation required for admission. Documents acuity, recommended level of care, and treatment plan.

consults.name: Social work
consults.result: SW to assist with means restriction, family contact, admission logistics
consults.reasoning: Social work critical for means restriction coordination, family involvement, and disposition planning.

disposition.name: Admit to inpatient psychiatric unit
disposition.result: Admission to locked psychiatric unit for safety, stabilization, and treatment initiation. Voluntary if patient agrees; involuntary if refuses.
disposition.reasoning: ONLY appropriate disposition. Active suicidal ideation with plan, intent, timeline, and preparatory behavior requires inpatient hospitalization. Outpatient is NOT safe.

disposition.name: Discharge home
disposition.reasoning: NEVER discharge a patient with active suicidal ideation with plan, intent, and timeline. This patient would likely die if sent home tonight.

actualDiagnosis.name: Major Depressive Disorder, Severe, with Active Suicidal Ideation with Plan and Intent
teachingPoints: ALWAYS ask directly about suicide - it does NOT increase risk
Risk assessment: Ideation → Plan → Means → Intent → Timeline
Means restriction is the #1 evidence-based intervention
Safety contracts have NO evidence of efficacy - do not rely on them
This patient requires inpatient psychiatric hospitalization
Involve family in means restriction (remove ALL firearms, medications)
Document your risk assessment thoroughly
High-risk features: Male, access to firearms, specific plan, recent preparatory behavior, hopelessness, substance use

cssrs.name: Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS)
cssrs.description: Structured assessment of suicidal ideation and behavior - gold standard for suicide risk documentation

ideation.description: Active SI with any methods (not plan) without intent
Active SI with some intent to act, without specific plan
Active SI with specific plan and intent

thisPatient.interpretation: HIGHEST RISK - Active SI with specific plan AND intent
thisPatient.behaviors: Preparatory behavior (giving away possessions, saying goodbye)
thisPatient.clinicalAction: Requires immediate psychiatric hospitalization. 1:1 observation. Means restriction STAT.

cssrs.note: C-SSRS is evidence-based and recommended by Joint Commission. Documents the clinical basis for admission decisions.
cssrs.source: Posner K et al. Am J Psychiatry. 2011;168(12):1266-1277

sadPersons.name: SAD PERSONS Scale
sadPersons.caveat: Clinical judgment always supersedes scores. This patient requires admission regardless of score.

isPathWarm.name: IS PATH WARM
isPathWarm.description: Warning signs mnemonic (American Association of Suicidology)

signs.meaning: Ideation - threatening or talking about suicide
Substance abuse - increased or new use

isPathWarm.note: These are WARNING SIGNS requiring immediate attention, not a scoring system

male-35-depression.correctDiagnosis: Major Depressive Disorder, Severe, with Suicidal Ideation
male-35-depression.teachingPoint: This is a psychiatric emergency. This patient requires inpatient psychiatric admission. Key points: 1) Ask directly and specifically about suicide, 2) Assess plan, means, intent, timeline, 3) Means restriction (removing firearms) saves lives, 4) Safety contracts don't work - don't rely on them, 5) Document your full risk assessment, 6) When in doubt, err on the side of safety - admit. The mnemonic 'IS PATH WARM' captures risk factors: Ideation, Substance abuse, Purposelessness, Anxiety, Trapped, Hopelessness, Withdrawal, Anger, Recklessness, Mood changes.
male-35-depression.clinicalPearl: The most important thing you can do is ASK. Many clinicians avoid asking about suicide, fearing they'll 'plant the idea.' This is a myth. Patients are often relieved when someone asks directly. Use clear language: 'Are you thinking about suicide?' not vague terms like 'hurting yourself.' And remember: means restriction works. If he doesn't have access to that gun, he's much more likely to survive this crisis.
